title: Chapter 17
text: Chapter Fourteen N othing appeared out of the ordinary when Ronin stepped out of his dwelling. It was another typical day in Cheyenne — the sky was a yellow-tinged gray, the wind blew steady at about ten miles per hour, and the temperature had already hit seventy-nine degrees. It would be in the mid-eighties by the afternoon. The park across the street was empty; quiet and green, alive but unchanging . The sound of the deadbolt sliding into place as he locked it was oddly pronounced, shattering the morning’s monotony . Her words from a week ago echoed from his memory . I’m sure that’ll do wonders to keep bots out . He pushed himself forward, through the gates and into the Market, and then south into the human slums. All the while, his sensors searched for something, anything , abnormal enough to justify his turning around. He noted the discrepancy in his stride — seventy-five percent of his normal pace — but he couldn’t bring himself to alter it . Countless calculations and simulations said that she’d be fine during his absence. The chances of anything happening were slim, so long as she remained indoors. She knew to hide in the attic, if any bots came knocking . The words from the journal skittered across his processors, and he abruptly shifted functions. Diagnostics checks, system analysis, rough mapping of his potential route; anything but revisiting what he had read . Ronin forced his attention to his surroundings. The humans’ homes were constructed from the rubble Warlord didn’t want — they were collections of sometimes conflicting materials, often in poor condition, that should never have held together. Yet, somehow, they stood in defiance of the pristine buildings within the wall, in defiance of the Dust and its furious storms . There was no elegance here, no precision or cleanliness. Just rugged determination. They’d taken what the bots had deemed trash and repurposed it with imagination and ingenuity . Some of the residents stopped to stare at him as he passed. They would know, if only by the state of his clothing, that he wasn’t one of them. No one spoke to him . Many of the humans kept small gardens of stubby, struggling crops — some in patches of dirt behind their shacks, others in cracked pottery, some in ancient tubs and sinks. A few residences had makeshift pens with pigs, goats, or chickens. It was a far cry from the fields outside of Cheyenne — Warlord kept the agricultural bots working, and traded their harvests to the humans — but it was something . It was surviving . These people didn’t need programming to dictate their day-to-day activities. They did whatever their situation called for, whether it was growing food, repairing a building, or battling an enemy . Lara made her own way. She found small pleasures where she could. Where she couldn’t, she gritted her teeth and pressed on . A mile south of the human slums, Ronin stopped. Without meaning to, he’d followed the route Lara had taken the day he’d made his offer to her — down Morrie, into the old residential ruins of southern Cheyenne. He saw it as it was now — the crumbling remains of houses where humans and bots had once dwelled in peace — and how it had been that day. Relentless rain, small rivers flowing in the cracked streets, puddles filling the breaks in the concrete . His memory replayed Lara, defeated and soaked, as she knelt over the storm drain at his feet. Her tears were indistinguishable from the rainwater . Ronin tilted his head, staring down at the clogged drain. A flash of reflected light caught his attention. He dropped to one knee, enhancing his optics to locate the source. Centuries of buildup cluttered the drain; chunks of concrete, scraps of tattered cloth, and broken pieces of wood . And there, lodged amidst the refuse, was a bit of gold . Holding the strap of his rifle in place with his left hand, he reached into the rubble with his right. He clawed through the dirt and debris. The clutter shifted, collapsing atop itself, and a cloud of dust filled the air. Ronin withdrew his arm and opened his fingers . He held a ring on his open palm. A slender gold band, free of adornment save the single diamond set on its outside. After brushing dirt from it, he took it between forefinger and thumb and raised it to the light . This was what she’d found, that day. What she’d lost. The ring was precious metal, and would fetch a high price at the Market. For Lara to have been so close to pulling herself out of squalor, only to trip and accidentally throw her salvation away … How would it have felt, if he’d held the key to his core programming in his hand and lost it a moment later ? He tucked the ring into his inside coat pocket and rose, swinging his optics toward Cheyenne. What would Lara think if he went back now and gave her the ring ? No. There was work to be done, and three days was already too short a time. She’d be there when Ronin returned, no matter what terrible outcomes his simulations suggested as possibilities . Ronin pulled on his mask, goggles, and gloves, and raised his hood . It was only three days . * * * I n the early years after Ronin’s reactivation, many of the old signs were still intact. He’d committed so many of them to memory — Nebraska, Iowa, Kansas, Dakota, Sioux City, Omaha, Amarillo. Dozens, hundreds, thousands of places. Those names were lost to time, now; everything was dust . There’d been a sign here, too. Welcome to colorful Colorado , it said. Only a single wood post remained, rotted and torn apart by insects. The nearby building was a pile of old roofing and crumbled bricks. Husks of vehicles lay half-buried in the dirt all around, but there was little left of value, unless he decided to drag a gutted car frame back to Cheyenne . He continued south along the road, following the sparse patches where dust hadn’t been able to settle. Coarse, brown grass jutted from cracks in the ruined pavement, swaying in the breeze. Another landscape that was everchanging, but never truly any different . This was what the people of Cheyenne had faced. This was the landscape they thought they’d be forced into. Mile upon mile of dirt and shifting dunes, with only the toughest vegetation clinging to a meager existence. This was why they’d fought . There was no question — the bot leader mentioned in the journal was Warlord. Had he been justified in his actions? Had it been a matter of survival, of his people against the humans ? Or had it been genocide ? Ronin cut west, toward the dark mountain peaks on the horizon. There were no answers to those questions out here. He’d ended more than his share of people, often without remorse; he couldn’t pass judgment . Still, didn’t such destruction run counter to what the Creators stood for? The humans described in the journal — a broken, frightened people, clinging to the remnants of their old lives — couldn’t have posed much threat to Warlord and his bots . Part of Ronin — gaining strength with each step — wanted only to return to Lara. She wasn’t entirely safe in Cheyenne, not while Warlord and his gearheads ruled, but there was more to it . There was a chance he’d eventually discover the nature of his programming out in the Dust. But with Lara, he might learn how to live . He passed the ruins marking the edge of Fort Collins as the sun sank toward the western horizon. The green grass and living trees were naturally occurring here, if still somewhat sparse, and might have attracted more settlers if not for its location. This was the edge of the dust, no-man’s land, too harsh to offer true prosperity . The lingering effects of the long-ago cataclysm farther south didn’t help . They say Denver’s just a radioactive crater . He’d gone there once, more than a hundred years ago. The devastation stretched for miles — buildings flattened, an entire city wiped out of existence. His sensors had picked up trace levels of radiation, even keeping his distance. It was no place for man or bot . Here, fifty miles away, entire towns had been spared such destruction. Decades of disrepair and constant scavenging had taken a toll, but places like this still held treasures . And dangers . He swung his rifle to his front, taking the worn grip comfortably into hand, and entered a copse of trees just off the road into town. Ronin had walked thousands of places like this. More than enough to know they weren’t always as empty as they appeared . Easing down on his belly in the brush, Ronin awaited nightfall . * * * A storm blew in with nightfall. Wind whipped through the deserted streets of Fort Collins, but the flashes of lightning were farther west, where dark clouds loomed over the mountains. There were lights in the hills — campfires, most likely — reminding Ronin that people did inhabit the area. Though the nearby rivers and lakes were far lower than they used to be, their water was an invaluable resource, and run-off from the mountains kept them fresh . He engaged night-vision as he entered town. The overgrown streets were empty, save for the husks of vehicles that had been stripped down to their frames long before. A surprising number of buildings were standing, though many had holes in walls and roofs. Waist-high stretches of wall and jutting steel supports were all that remained upright in some places . Ronin compared the place to its state during his last visit, 5,023 days before. Several more walls had collapsed, particularly on the brick structures, and the vegetation had spread, resulting in more damage . Rifle at the ready, he entered one of the buildings. He moved slowly, silently, and listened through the howling wind for any sound betraying the presence of another person. Though the search remained his primary function, his background processes turned toward Lara . Should he have taken her with him? He hadn’t lied about the danger, but at least he would’ve been near to defend her, if the need arose. If something happened in Cheyenne while Ronin was gone, she was all but helpless . He froze, a piece of rotting plywood forgotten in his hand. He’d left Lara vulnerable. How could he abandon her, knowing the history of that city? Knowing what had happened to the people who lived in those houses ? One of the journal entries surged up from his memory, words a shaky scrawl. There’d been a bloody fingerprint on the bottom right corner . I was going to head down to the bar again tonight. Don’t know why. The booze is sour and the company is bleak, but something’s been brewing there, and I ain’t talking about beer. People really worked themselves up these last few days, and I can’t blame them. I know it was stupid — hell, I knew it long before tonight — but I can’t blame them at all . I saw them from down 19 th , filling up the street. God, it was almost like those old movies, when all the villagers took up pitchforks and torches, only here it was shotguns and hunting rifles, crowbars and baseball bats. They were gathered up on the corner, shouting, and those bots…they just lined up across the street from the bar, shoulder to shoulder, and stood there. Didn’t move, didn’t say a goddamn word . Shit escalated. Shit fucking escalated . The bot that leads them stepped up. Told everyone to get their shit and go home. Someone threw a rock at it . Those things didn’t even bother wasting bullets. Didn’t need to. Most of them look human, but fuck…they tore through those people like humans are made of paper. Crowd fought for maybe thirty seconds, and then their nerve broke. Took off in all directions, a lot of them coming towards me. I helped Julio Ortega get around the corner. He wasn’t with his family when they got taken. When he let go of his stomach , his … God, his blood is all over me. I can’t. Can’t write this anymore . I don’t think they’re searching houses yet. Guess they’re content to slaughter people like cattle in the street, and that’s enough tonight. Like it was nothing … Ronin’s urge to return home was more powerful than ever, but it wouldn’t do any good; it was hours back to Cheyenne, even if he was quick, and the trip would’ve been wasted energy. He’d come all the way out here. He had to do what he’d intended . He searched the smaller buildings as the night progressed. Occasionally, their interiors lit up, followed several seconds later by wall-rumbling thunder. Bits of scrap metal and plastic accumulated in his rucksack. It would only be a small fraction of his last haul. The gold ring could up his return significantly, but it couldn’t be counted; it was Lara’s. Whether it should be traded was her choice . As dawn neared, the storm cleared out. Ronin moved into the larger structures near the town center. These showed more damage than many of the others — due, at least in part, to their size — but they were navigable . In the first, he discovered the remains of three humans — skeletons in ragged clothing. The fingers of the largest were wrapped around the grip of a rusted revolver. Ronin knelt, brushing away the dirt on the floor to reveal the message painted there . Saw their faces Heard their screams No one left to absolve us our sins He contemplated those words as minutes passed and the sun crept higher over the eastern horizon. Would they hold significance to Lara? Ronin could almost piece meaning together, could almost understand . Weren’t there images in his memory he couldn’t erase? Screams echoing from his otherwise forgotten past? Explosions and charging armies, gunshots and blood and oil … Ronin forced himself to stand and left the bodies behind. It would do him no good to pursue the fragments of another life . He went into the building with MNICIL — probably MUNICIPAL , before the Black Out — over the entrance. Sections of the ceiling had collapsed, likely due to decades of water damage, and most of the interior furnishings had been reduced to useless scraps. He worked his way deeper inside, clearing some of the rubble from a hallway to access another section . Debris blanketed the floor; rotted bits of plaster, shards of glass, shreds of dingy carpet, and pieces of unidentifiable furniture. A padlocked door stood at the end of the hall. The heavy lock gave way with a single blow, and the entire door came off its hinges when Ronin pulled it open. Inside stood three service bots, one with a broom in hand, their once shining casings dulled with age and dust . The ceiling was reinforced, showing no signs of rot. Ronin had encountered rooms like this one in other places. Were there rules, in the old world, about the sorts of rooms bots had to be stored in ? Stepping inside, he searched the shelves lining the walls, stuffing six long-dead power cells and several specialized tools into his bag. A small box held miscellaneous parts, unidentifiable because of the built-up dust and lint. He placed the entire thing in his bag, atop the other items, but he left the bots alone . Once, they’d moved and worked and spoken and thought. Even if their functions were simple, they’d been alive . Returning to the hallway, Ronin crouched and rummaged through his bag, checking his haul. The tools and assorted parts would undoubtedly bring in a stack of credit, but the power cells were the real treasure. Even uncharged, they alone were likely to bring in as much as his entire last haul . When Ronin emerged from the building, the sun was at its zenith, shrouded by the gray of the sky. The haze was often mistaken for clouds, but Ronin knew if for what it was. The Dust wasn’t just beneath his boots; it was everywhere, over and under, outside and inside. Even with his skin fully replaced, it was only a matter of time before it built up in his internal mechanisms, before it caused internal failures and his eventual breakdown. No one — whether bot or human — escaped the Dust . Not even within the walls of Cheyenne . Lara was still alone. Was she frightened? Upset ? Lonely ? Perhaps she was brimming with anger, because Ronin hadn’t said goodbye; he had departed while she slept . He didn’t want to dwell on regrets. No, if he was never to see Lara again — and he couldn’t dismiss that possibility, even as he railed against it — he wanted to remember her saying she would wait for him. The look in her eyes had spoken the truth. She would actually miss him . His plan had been to scavenge until just before dawn on the third day, to maximize his time here. There was more to find, he knew, and hundreds of thousands of square feet to cover in these buildings . But Lara was alone . Traveling during midday — especially when entering or leaving known scavenging spots like this — was dangerous. It left him exposed. In the open Dust, reliable hiding places were difficult to come by. The decaying buildings of Fort Collins, however, provided ample cover for would-be ambushers. There was no guarantee that he hadn’t been spotted by the hill- dwellers . The safest course would be to shelter in one of the buildings, wait out nightfall, and leave under cover of darkness . Sensors pulsed on his cheek, and he scratched at it absently. Would six or seven more hours make a difference? Undoubtedly. But which effect was more important — the one on his haul, or the one on the woman waiting for him in Cheyenne ? For all the years he’d been awake, this was a new experience, an unexplored dilemma. He’d never had anyone waiting for him. Not a bot, not a human, not anything. There’d been no companionship. His interactions had been limited to passing conversation, negotiations over trades, the occasional night with a female, and the even rarer peaceful encounter in the wastes . He cinched the top of his bag, closed the flap, and slung it over his shoulders. As he stood, he slipped his right arm through the strap of his rifle. His hand settled on the familiar grip, thumb sliding to the indentation worn over decades of use. Glancing down, he performed an inventory of his hand tools, ensuring they were all in place . The trees would provide some cover while he made his way out of town. Beyond that, he’d be back in the Dust, visible from miles away. At least he’d have the same field of view . * * * T hough the sun was still in the sky, the lights of Cheyenne were on as Ronin approached from the eastern railway, which connected to the path between the Bot District and human slums. He’d followed the north road out of Fort Collins. Varying his route helped avoid potential ambushes on the return trip . The railyard had only been partially cleared in all its years of disuse. Hulking train cars, metal and wood having been stripped from many of their frames, lay silent amidst the dunes that had gathered around them. A few were in jumbled heaps of twisted, scorched metal, covered in rust and grime . Movement caught his eye ahead, just off the path. He enhanced his optics, zooming in on the source. A murder of crows, picking at a carcass; their ragged caws drifted to him on the westbound wind just as he obtained a visual . The birds’ meal became more apparent as Ronin neared. His steps slowed. The crows continued their calls, jerking their heads aside to stare at him with black eyes and bits of flesh dangling from their beaks . He surged forward, startling the creatures into scattering. They regrouped on a nearby train car to watch him . Time lost meaning as he stared at what the birds had been feeding upon. Five minutes and seven seconds ticked by much too fast, and Ronin was so still that some of the crows returned, hopping warily closer until he waved his arm to scare them off again . It was the corpse of a human woman. She was naked, and where her skin hadn’t been torn apart by hungry beaks, it was dark with bruises. His first thought was that Warlord had found Lara, but he rejected it immediately. This woman’s skin was a darker shade, her black hair stiff with blood . A synth lay on the ground near her, his head face-down over her crotch. His detached arms and legs were strewn about on all sides, tendrils of exposed wiring and tubing jutting from the jagged openings. Warlord’s symbol was upon his back; the paint had run before drying . The synth’s penis had been removed, as well, and shoved into the woman’s mouth. Crows had pecked out her eyes, but her collapsed cheekbone, too-wide jaw, and extensive bruising indicated that she’d been severely beaten before she was dumped here . She was a little taller than Lara, with full hips and breasts. Dark hair, tan skin … Ronin’s processors slowed as realization hit him, stronger than any wall of dust he’d endured in the wastes . The woman’s arms were spread to either side, palms up. Slowly, he knelt beside her, took hold of her left wrist, and turned her hand over . A jagged scar ran along it’s back, from the first knuckle of her pinky to the base of her thumb . As delicately as he could, he set her hand back down. His processors kicked into overdrive, moving fast enough that anyone nearby might have heard them whirring. He’d fulfilled one of Lara’s conditions. One less thing for him to ask about around town. One less suspicion to raise with Warlord . He stood and looked over his shoulder at the crows. They remained close, cawing and flapping their wings as they fought over whatever bits of meat they still possessed. They’d desecrated the body of Lara’s sister. Damaged it beyond what it had already suffered. Ronin looked down again. No…this wasn’t an it . This was Tabitha. A her . The synth’s facial skin was gone, leaving only dull, dented metal and shattered optics. Ronin moved the bot aside, piling the detached parts on the ground near its torso. That done, he removed the final insult from Tabitha’s mouth and gathered her in his arms; he’d see to her first, to keep the scavengers at bay . He walked west, searching for a resting place outside of Warlord’s shadow .
